tree._: Volume 3 presents Ecosquared, an alternative economic operating system accessible through the Sqale platform, which combines vector-money with gratitude-tracking to create social accountability through high-trust networks. By stripping money of the illusion that it goes backwards (to the supply chain who produced a thing), collaborative intentions become transparent, relationships enliven, and the psycho-social pollution of three thousand years of exchange-based economics begins to dissolve — shifting civilisation from ownership to stewardship, from competition to collaboration, from dead money to living money.

1._: The verso explores our everyday experience of money — what it is, what it does to us psycho-socially, and what an alternative might feel like — presenting the Sqale platform's power usecases and the emergent experience economic that grows from the simple act of aligning money with the intent and responsibility of sharing.

We all grew up in a gift economy: as children everything was provided — food, shelter, love — and money was an in-between thing with no intrinsic value. The coin was taken from parents and given to shopkeepers to get ice-cream; what we valued was the taste, the joy. For an alternative to money to work, it must be as simple as this: children should find it natural.

Traditional money is dead money — it does nothing in your wallet or bank. The $10 remains $10 no matter how useful it has been, however many times it has changed hands. The moving, living part goes untracked. When buying coffee, you pay the same whether local regular or stranger; money deadens the relationship. Living money would reflect our living relationships — personal recommendation, ongoing investment in people and places.

Money is like oil: useful but with unintentional negative side-effects. The pollution is psycho-social — three thousand years of exchange have created our current global state of social ill-health, a market of the corners of the world much depleted. 'Zero Trust' is advertised as a positive business goal, not collaboration. 'Non-growth' is lauded rather than de-mechanisation.
1.1: If the value-metrics of Ecosquared work better than money — no advertising, stable trust-metric — why would anyone convert back? Converting traditional money altogether into a value-economic: not laundering dirty money into clean, but transforming the entire monetary system. A world with no money, just comparative value.

2._: Negative money: the knotty problem underlying the psycho-social dynamic. Three forms of negativity compound into a self-reinforcing knot that locks humanity into competitive, anxious, ownership-based existence.

1._: First negative: at the point of exchange, money is given for a thing with the delusion it goes back to producers — but the farmer has already been paid; the shopkeeper's money goes forward to their child's trombone lesson. Money actually moves forward. Reversing the arrow of time creates psychological negativity and cashflow problems in finance.

Second negative: people work for money to pay for housing, food, entertainment — money is 'positive', departing with it is 'negative'. People become locked into work because of responsibilities to dependents, fear of losing income. Third negative: before the 18th century, debt was personal and relational; once negative numbers stabilised in mathematics, personal debts were added together impersonally, enabling banks to issue loans without actual money — opening the weakness of bank runs.

Cumulative effect: 99% of money in circulation is negative. The world runs on debt (negative 3), people locked into working to pay mortgages (negative 2), selling time when they don't want to (consequence of negative 1). A more subtle negative: buyer and seller in opposition — mathematically only ¼ leave thinking both benefit. Multiplied over centuries, markets generate landscapes of social stress.
1.1: Finally, ownership itself is negative in the sense of imaginary (see V-1). The £1 paid for a pen means ownership is associated with the absent £1. People continue paying for houses despite original builders having long since passed. By contrast, Ecosquared has no negative — no exchange, therefore no illusion of money going backward.

3._: Sqale power usecases emerge from the simple act of aligning money with the intent/responsibility of sharing. Instead of exchange, vector-money accompanies a thing when shared; number-value tracks subjective evaluations. Users buy credits ($10 = 1000 credits) setting up one-to-one relationship between traditional money and vector-money.

1._: Organic Sharing / Virality: Alice gives Bob a music track with $1-equivalent. Bob shares forward to Charlie, adds $1 of his own to Cam. If everyone matches and shares to two people within five minutes: within one hour $1000-equivalent in flow, two hours $1m, three hours $1b. Naturally enables crowd-funding — after sufficient sharing, originators invite Support instead of further sharing.
1.1: Social Neuron: instead of virality, the aim is connecting two people. Alice shares her CV with $1000 reward for helpers. Friends share forward until employers respond. On employment, reward splits equally along the specific chain of people who connected them. Reinforcement works like dopamine release when neurons connect — the social success of matching people reinforces good will in sharing.

3.2: Value-Tracking & Silent Applause: evaluations of meetings, events, performances. Silent Applause projects live fluctuations behind a speaker — unlike a twitter-stream, numbers indicate response the host can react to without interrupting flow. Fair-Share: vector-money attracted to a product/service immediately distributes to the team according to ongoing evaluations between members. Golden Ticket: public sharing links with random credit allocation — high-value 'golden tickets' amongst standard invitations.

4._: The emergent experience economic evolves through six phases, each named by its primary characteristic, each contrasted with its traditional equivalent and each mapped to another Fulcrum volume: gift/sharing (vs exchange), network (vs centralised, V2), instant/transparent (vs delayed tax/closed accounts, V1), trust (vs zero-trust, V0), value (vs political cycles, V-1), organic (vs impersonal mechanics, V-2), experience (vs machine-like production, V-3).

1._: Over time, vector-money works more like an empowered vote directing resources toward future objectives. The Projection Map indicates social health — how far into the future we are preparing, ensuring food security for days through centuries. Transparent decentralised civic planning: everyone's intentions backed by vector-money, visible from street lighting to national projects.
1.1: Since there is a one-to-one relationship between money and credits, what does the money sitting in a bank do while credits circulate on Sqale? It could be secured in long-term investment — land and housing. Every pound converted to credits could count as partial collective ownership. Why own a house when you can time-share a pool of residencies globally?

5._: Environmental stats as mirror of social health: a third of land vertebrate species lost by 2050, 69% global biodiversity decline since 1970, CO2 highest in 4 million years (420ppm from 310ppm in 1960), 15.3 billion trees felled annually, Antarctic/Greenland ice melting at 420 billion tons/year. The environment problems are effects — symptoms of our social problem.
5.1: The question of temporal interpretation: a 20-year-old and 50-year-old bring different experience to these numbers. The book sets change within a generation — 20-25 years — the maximum horizon a young person conceives, the absolute minimum an elder might agree upon.

1.6: Origins: 2012 at the Hub in Piccadilly, London, during an interview about the future of work. The throwaway comment 'double your money' — if visiting the Hub doubled your money, everyone would come but by invitation only. Combined with actually leaving with more money that day. Over a month, developed the Money-Time-Trust-Protocol which appeared to create an 'economic entity' — Ecosquared. Two years to peel apart MTTP and realise it treated money as a vector in time between people, stabilising into the simpler protocol of organic sharing: giving a thing with money.

2._: The recto establishes Ecosquared as ecological economic through formal comparison with traditional economics, academic triangulation via Knight's uncertainty problem and Polanyi's Great Transformation, the mathematical architecture of scalar values / vector money / collective tensor / whole-system manifold, and the SQ algorithm as non-centralised means of fair distribution — changing the axioms of money at source, 3000 years of disruption versus blockchain's 400.

1._: The fundamental: uncertainty can be dealt with by treating money as a vector. The market loses volatility when we know where money is going. Mathematicians use vectors to track dynamic systems (fluid, water, air); traditional money is scalar and hence cannot accurately track movement as currency. Vector-money has direction and is only ever owned by people, never organisations.

Comparing operating systems: traditional economics tracks the point of exchange (money for thing), compensating inherent uncertainty with contracts, insurance, legal systems, imprisonment. Ecosquared tracks relational intention-responsibility (thing given with credits), generating stability through transparent direction of intent and default trust.

Key structural differences: traditional money creates markets based on competition ($600b+ spent on digital advertising alone in 2021, 15-79% of internet bandwidth delivering ads, 300 TWh annually devoted to cybersecurity of distrust). Ecosquared creates meshworks of sharing — no point of exchange therefore no trade, no debt (no negative numbers), no ownership of money by organisations.
1.1: Since vector-money is tagged to time, more moneyflow enables longer-term projects. Trust is placed in people who share forward, not in money which can buy anything irrespective of relationship. Gaming the system means gaming known people personally, who can withhold sharing in return. Relational accountability replaces institutional enforcement.

2._: Academic triangulation: Knight (1921) identified the forward-looking uncertainty problem at the bottom of economics. Keynes (1933/1937) recognised the limits of mathematical probability applied to human behaviour — 'About these matters there is no scientific basis on which to form any calculable probability whatever. We simply do not know!' Knight warned against 'the transfer into the human sciences of the concepts and products of the sciences of nature' (1927).

1._: Polanyi's Great Transformation (1944) argues labour, land, and money are 'fictitious commodities' — labour is not a commodity but a necessary factor of human existence; land is a finite resource whose commodification creates unprecedented misery; money is a social institution not a natural commodity. Polanyi called for axiological requalification of the economic system; little has changed since.
1.1: The social coordination problem (Clower 1984, Paniagua 2018 via Polanyi 1951) is grounded in 'market decision-making' rather than resource sharing. By altering the central axioms of money, we expand concepts beyond the traditional economic: 'money' becomes 'accounting system', 'social complexity' becomes 'network of informal embodied relations', 'knowledge' becomes 'wisdom, awareness, attention'. Put simply, there is no market.

3._: The mathematical architecture operates across the vertical of factor, vector, tensor, manifold. Factor-value is a scalar enabling any individual to evaluate intuitively 1-10. Vector-money funds future projects. Collection of vectors toward an objective maps a tensor in psycho-social space. The combined tensor map of all nested projects indicates the meshwork's social health — an alternative social manifold, Ecological Economics.

1._: Three subjective phases: evaluating past things (an invitation to internal evaluating, the thing doesn't change), evaluating future projects (attitude influences outcome, interpretable as hope or intention), evaluating people (inherently reflexive — ratings don't work, but reflexive evaluation supported by unbiased arithmetic average cannot be gamed). Vector-money aligns past products with present people with future projects as sharing, inviting, investing.

The SQ algorithm: a fractional relative value algorithm similar to Google PageRank, purely arithmetic with no verbal bias. Andrew evaluates teammates: 4 for Bella, 6 for Charlie, 10 for Cathy → 20%/30%/50% fractional distribution. Iterative calculation continues until the ratio of sums-received to sums-given approaches 1.0 — an elegant indicator of social balance.
1.1: SQ distributes resources non-centrally: vector-money directed to a project distributes immediately to aligned individuals (not a holding company) proportionally by SQ. Also usable as trust-metric for governance — a person with SQ of 2 has social power of two people. Threshold percentages enable any political structure from equal democracy to weighted democracy, autocracy to consensus.

4._: Intrinsic value: the act of sharing is intrinsically value-sourced, unlike exchange where money becomes proxy for human value, susceptible to manipulation and corruption. Money is military tech — tokens stamped with the emperor's profile enabling imperial logistics — from royal tithes to governmental taxes to banks charging interest to companies taking profit. Whatever the activity, an amount is extracted.
4.1: With credits, vector-money aligns relational value: sharing-power replaces buying-power (the more shared the more entrusted). Agency shifts from effector (cog in machine, selling time for money) to effect-cause actuator (reception of gift converted into offering). Power-over becomes power-with (V-2). Ownership becomes stewardship — as money converts to vector-money, positional salary hierarchy becomes arrowhead of empowered purpose.

5._: Hard cash: Sqale generates traditional revenue through an inverted taxation ramp (32% at <10K users, halving per order of magnitude to 0% at 1 billion) plus direct support through the platform itself. Money converted to credits sits in a holding account — the lump sum may accrue interest, buy collective assets, or liberate land from ownership.
5.1: Ultimately there is no Sqale company which needs to make money — credits are not held by any bounded organisation. The implications potentially invert all institutional structures. A global version costs $500k, preferably funded from viral success to avoid legacy ownership entailed by venture funding. Every conversion of money to credits shifts the world from exchange to sharing.
